before: API keys are sufficient as the default integration posture across major SDKs.
after: Workload identity and OIDC federation are becoming the production-default posture where available.
evidence_span: Recent SDK and platform docs across OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, and AWS emphasize keyless/cloud identity patterns.
confidence_basis: Primary-source docs and release guidance converging on same auth direction.
falsifier: Major providers reverse guidance and re-center long-lived API keys as preferred production defaults.
hallucination_risk: low — direct primary docs and repositories

before: Semver alone is enough to safely automate upgrades in SDK-heavy codebases.
after: Behavior and typing shifts can still produce production risk; pinned lanes and staged rollouts are required.
evidence_span: Multiple SDK ecosystems document caveats around defaults, types, and upgrade behavior.
confidence_basis: Cross-vendor consistency in caveat patterns, with explicit provider documentation.
falsifier: Critical SDKs maintain strict no-surprise behavior across several release cycles with no meaningful caveat notes.
residual_disagreement: Exact strictness varies by vendor and package family.

before: Runtime correctness in modern web stacks depends mostly on component code quality.
after: Runtime correctness depends equally or more on explicit framework directives and boundaries.
evidence_span: Next.js cache/runtime directives, Nuxt data-fetching rules, and Astro hydration directives define correctness and perf outcomes.
confidence_basis: Official framework guidance directly maps to common production defects.
falsifier: Framework teams deprecate these directives and return to primarily implicit behavior.

before: Cross-platform mobile selection is mainly about developer productivity and team familiarity.
after: Migration readiness and architecture transition risk are co-equal selection criteria.
evidence_span: RN/Expo architecture transitions and Flutter renderer evolution changed upgrade risk profiles.
confidence_basis: Architecture transitions create recurring compatibility and delivery friction.
falsifier: Large mobile framework upgrades consistently deliver without compatibility lag for ecosystem packages.
residual_disagreement: Risk severity varies by app-native dependency footprint.

before: Platform lock-in is always the dominant risk in choosing framework/SDK stacks.
after: Operational ambiguity in defaults/upgrades can be equally damaging even in open-source stacks.
evidence_span: Incidents often derive from implicit retries/caching/version shifts, not just hosting or vendor lock-in.
confidence_basis: Pattern emerges across ecosystems; quantitative incident-rate comparison still incomplete.
falsifier: Operational incidents remain overwhelmingly attributable to lock-in constraints rather than default behavior ambiguity.
residual_disagreement: Need incident dataset across multiple organizations to settle impact weighting.
hallucination_risk: medium — comparative weighting not fully measured
